content.text:
There's the actual error — buried under the "no-thin" one earlier. The target is on **HTTPS**, not HTTP:

```
http 400: ... Client sent an HTTP request to an HTTPS server.
```

That's the Go HTTP server's stock message when a TLS listener gets a cleartext request. Quick check:

```bash
# What's actually listening?
curl -sI http://localhost:8080/test/kubernetes-mirror.git/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack
curl -skI https://localhost:8080/test/kubernetes-mirror.git/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack
```

Then switch the URL and, for a local self-signed cert, skip TLS verify:

```bash
TGT=https://localhost:8080/test/kubernetes-mirror.git
go run ./cmd/git-sync probe --json \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  $SRC $TGT | jq '.target.capabilities'
```

## About the misleading "no-thin" error

Worth flagging in the repo: when the target's receive-pack `info/refs` fails with an HTTP error *and* we can't produce a capability advertisement, replicate currently surfaces `replicate-target-no-thin` because the `RelayTargetPolicy{CapabilitiesKnown: false, NoThin: false}` zero value means `SupportsReplicateRelay` trips the `no-thin` check after the `CapabilitiesKnown` check. Actually hmm — let me think about that again.

Actually never mind — the earlier "no-thin" was probably from a different target (your shell `TGT` may have changed, or the first run used a different server that's been replaced). The error message in your last output is unambiguous: **the server at `localhost:8080` is TLS-only**, so `http://` hits a TLS listener and gets rejected before the replicate logic ever runs.

Fix the URL scheme and retry:

```bash
TGT=https://localhost:8080/test/kubernetes-mirror.git
go run ./cmd/git-sync probe --json \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  $SRC $TGT | jq '.target'
```

If that works, then run the replicate flow with the same flag and a target token (receive-pack on `entire` almost certainly requires auth):

```bash
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --target-token <your-token> \
  --stats --measure-memory --json \
  $SRC $TGT | jq .
```

If probe still errors, paste the output and I'll dig in.

message.content: export TGT=https://localhost:8080/git/test/git-sync-smoke-10

content.text:
Run:

```bash
go run ./cmd/git-sync probe --json \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --target-token "$ENTIRE_TOKEN" \
  $SRC $TGT | jq '{target_url: .target.url, target_caps: .target.capabilities}'
```

If that returns real capabilities, replicate should work with the same flags. If it 401s, the token is the gap; if it 404s, the repo path/ownership is off; if it reports back caps without `no-thin`, you're good to plan/replicate.

message.content: but again: the local target requires auth, how should that work?

content.text:
Auth flows through the same flag/env pairs for source and target independently. For your local `entire` target, you'll want to figure out what kind of credential it expects at the git smart-HTTP layer, then pass it as target auth. The three options git-sync supports:

## Option A — basic auth (username + token)

Most git hosts accept a PAT as password with any username. On `entire`:

```bash
export GITSYNC_TARGET_USERNAME=<your-user>
export GITSYNC_TARGET_TOKEN=<entire-pat>
# or inline:
--target-username <user> --target-token <token>
```

This sets HTTP Basic `Authorization: Basic base64(user:token)`.

## Option B — bearer token

If `entire` issues OAuth/JWT-style tokens for git HTTP:

```bash
export GITSYNC_TARGET_BEARER_TOKEN=<bearer>
# or:
--target-bearer-token <bearer>
```

Sets `Authorization: Bearer <bearer>`.

## Option C — programmatic (if embedding)

Not relevant here — that's `AuthProvider` on the `gitsync` library.

## How to figure out which one `entire` wants

```bash
# Try authed probe with basic first:
go run ./cmd/git-sync probe --json \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --target-username test \
  --target-token <password-or-pat> \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | head -20
```

If `entire` uses the default test credentials (from `CLAUDE.md`: `test` / `password1234`), try those as `--target-username test --target-token password1234`. If it wants an OAuth token, generate one via the `entire.io` UI (or the `.default-oauth-clients` file) and try as bearer.

## Source auth if needed

For the kubernetes/linux source side, public HTTPS doesn't need auth — but GitHub rate-limits anonymous. If you start seeing slow/erratic fetches:

```bash
--source-token <github-pat>
```

## Full command once you've nailed the target auth

```bash
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --target-username test \
  --target-token password1234 \
  --stats --measure-memory --json \
  $SRC $TGT | jq .
```

## Sanity-check with raw git first

If you're not sure which auth mode works, try a plain git push to the target with a scratch repo — if that works, mirror the same credential style into `git-sync`:

```bash
# Basic:
git ls-remote https://test:password1234@localhost:8080/git/test/git-sync-smoke-10

# Bearer (via custom header — git itself doesn't do this natively, but tells you if the endpoint accepts it):
curl -sk -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" \
  https://localhost:8080/git/test/git-sync-smoke-10/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack | head -5
```

Whichever works at the curl/git level, set the matching git-sync flags.

message.content:
❯   go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
    --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
    --target-username test \
    --target-token password1234 \
    --stats --measure-memory --json \
    $SRC $TGT | jq .
error: list target refs: http 401: https://localhost:8080/git/test/git-sync-smoke-10/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack Invalid token format
exit status 1

content.text:
"Invalid token format" is `entire` saying: "I parsed the Authorization header, but the credential isn't in the shape I want." It's expecting a real access token, not `password1234`. The test user's password only works at the login UI — the git HTTP endpoint wants an OAuth/personal access token.

## Fastest path: get a personal access token and use bearer

```bash
# 1. Log in to the entire.io frontend as test/password1234 at http://localhost:5173/login
# 2. Generate a personal access token (or OAuth access token) via the UI
# 3. Use it as a bearer:

export GITSYNC_TARGET_BEARER_TOKEN=<token-from-ui>
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --stats --measure-memory --json \
  $SRC $TGT | jq .
```

If basic auth with the token-as-password works on other tools, try:

```bash
--target-username <user> --target-token <access-token>
```

## If you're not sure what token format entire wants

Confirm at the HTTP layer before involving `git-sync`:

```bash
# Bearer:
curl -skI -H "Authorization: Bearer $ENTIRE_TOKEN" \
  "$TGT/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack"

# Basic with token-as-password:
curl -skI -u "test:$ENTIRE_TOKEN" \
  "$TGT/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack"
```

Whichever returns `200 OK`, use the matching git-sync flags. If both return 401, the token itself is wrong (expired, wrong scope, wrong user).

## Where to look in `entire` code

If you want to see exactly what format the git smart-HTTP endpoint expects, the "Invalid token format" message is coming from `entire`'s auth middleware. Search:

```bash
grep -rn "Invalid token format" ../../entire/
```

That'll tell you which auth handler is rejecting the credential and what shape it wants.

message.content: I need to use the git auth helper somehow as we did before with the sync command

content.text:
The credential helper kicks in **only when no explicit auth flags are set**. Looking at `internal/auth/auth.go:22`:

> Order: explicit flags → Entire DB token → git credential helper → anonymous

So drop the `--target-username`/`--target-token` flags and it'll fall through to `git credential fill`:

```bash
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --stats --measure-memory --json \
  $SRC $TGT | jq .
```

## Verify the helper actually has a credential for this host

Before running, check that `git credential fill` returns something for your target:

```bash
printf 'protocol=https\nhost=localhost:8080\npath=git/test/git-sync-smoke-10\n\n' \
  | git credential fill
```

It should print back `username=...` and `password=...` lines. If it just echoes your input (no password), the helper has no credential cached for `localhost:8080` and you need to seed it:

```bash
# Easiest: make git cache it by doing one real operation
git ls-remote https://localhost:8080/git/test/git-sync-smoke-10
# Enter creds when prompted — your configured helper (osxkeychain, etc.) will store them.

# Or approve directly:
printf 'protocol=https\nhost=localhost:8080\npath=git/test/git-sync-smoke-10\nusername=<user>\npassword=<token>\n\n' \
  | git credential approve
```

## For TLS skip verification

The helper path itself doesn't know about `--target-insecure-skip-tls-verify`, but that flag is applied at the HTTP transport level independently — it still works alongside the credential helper. Keep the flag.

## If git ls-remote fails on TLS verify

Your shell's git needs `-c http.sslVerify=false` for localhost too:

```bash
git -c http.sslVerify=false ls-remote https://localhost:8080/git/test/git-sync-smoke-10
```

Once that prompt succeeds and caches, `git-sync replicate` without target auth flags will pick it up.

message.content:
❯ go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
    --target-username git --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
    --stats --measure-memory --json \
    $SRC $TGT | jq .
error: replicate requires relay-capable target: replicate-target-no-thin; use sync instead
exit status 1

hmm but no-thin would have popped up before when I used sync with kubernetes?

content.text:
Correct instinct — `sync` wouldn't have shown this because **sync tolerates `no-thin` and falls back to the materialized strategy**. Replicate is relay-only by design, so it refuses.

Here's the capability matrix:

| Strategy | Relay? | Tolerates `no-thin`? |
|---|---|---|
| `sync` (normal) | Yes (incremental-relay) when target supports thin | No — falls back to materialized |
| `sync --force` | No | N/A — always materialized |
| `bootstrap` | Yes | No — falls back to materialized |
| `replicate` | **Yes, always** | **No — hard error** |

So this is expected for replicate against a `no-thin` target. Two real questions:

## 1. Does entire actually advertise `no-thin`?

Confirm with the raw advertisement:

```bash
git credential fill <<<'protocol=https
host=localhost:8080
path=git/test/git-sync-smoke-10

' | {
  read _u; user=${_u#*=}
  read _p; pass=${_p#*=}
  curl -sk -u "$user:$pass" \
    "$TGT/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack" \
  | strings | grep -o 'no-thin\|ofs-delta\|report-status\|side-band-64k\|delete-refs' | sort -u
}
```

If `no-thin` is in the output, entire is genuinely advertising it. Most git servers don't — `go-git`'s own receive-pack server does advertise it in certain paths, and `entire` likely inherits that.

## 2. Is this a real limitation or an entire bug?

Thin packs (where some objects referenced by the pack live only on the target, not in the pack itself) are well-established and all mainstream servers support them. If entire advertises `no-thin`, it's either:

- **Intentional** — entire's storage layer can't resolve thin references. Then replicate genuinely can't work against this target and you should either fix entire or use `sync` here.
- **Overly conservative** — entire copied a default that doesn't reflect its actual capability. Worth removing.

Search:

```bash
grep -rn '"no-thin"\|NoThin\|capability\.NoThin' ../../entire/
```

## Quick workaround to test replicate logic end-to-end

If you just want to validate the replicate code path locally without entire, run against another local git server that advertises thin. Easiest is `git daemon` or a scratch Gitea container:

```bash
# Scratch local target via go-git's smart-http test server is overkill.
# Simplest: use gitea in docker:
docker run -d --name gitea -p 3000:3000 gitea/gitea:latest
# Create a repo via its UI at http://localhost:3000, then:
TGT=http://localhost:3000/<user>/<repo>.git
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate --stats --json $SRC $TGT | jq .
```

Want me to look at entire's git server implementation and see what's actually setting the advertisement?

That's a heredoc indent issue — the lines have leading spaces from your terminal. `git credential fill` is strict: keys must start at column 0. Try:

```bash
git credential fill <<EOF
protocol=https
host=localhost:8080
path=git/test/git-sync-smoke-10

EOF
```

Or avoid the heredoc entirely:

```bash
printf 'protocol=https\nhost=localhost:8080\npath=git/test/git-sync-smoke-10\n\n' \
  | git credential fill \
  | awk -F= '$1=="username"{u=$2} $1=="password"{p=$2} END{print u":"p}' \
  | { IFS= read up; curl -sk -u "$up" "$TGT/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack"; } \
  | strings \
  | grep -oE 'no-thin|ofs-delta|report-status|side-band-64k|delete-refs' \
  | sort -u
```

Or skipping the cred helper plumbing entirely, just paste the credential once:

```bash
read -s -p 'token: ' TOK; echo
curl -sk -u "git:$TOK" "$TGT/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack" \
  | strings \
  | grep -oE 'no-thin|ofs-delta|report-status|side-band-64k|delete-refs' \
  | sort -u
```

Expected output if entire advertises `no-thin`:

```
delete-refs
no-thin
ofs-delta
report-status
side-band-64k
```

If `no-thin` is missing from the output, then something else is producing the error and we should look at the RelayTargetPolicy construction in `git-sync`.

content.text:
Two things:

**1.** Your password has a typo: `passwrod1234`. That's likely why they're confusing you. Either you typed it wrong in the prompt, or it's been cached wrong.

**2.** Let's skip `git credential fill` entirely and just hit the endpoint directly so we can see the real advertisement. Give it the correct password:

```bash
curl -sk -u 'test:password1234' \
  "$TGT/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack" \
  -o /tmp/tgt-advert.bin

# HTTP status line:
curl -sk -u 'test:password1234' -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' \
  "$TGT/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack"

# First ~300 bytes (the advert begins with "001f# service=git-receive-pack" then caps line):
head -c 300 /tmp/tgt-advert.bin | strings

# Just the caps you care about:
strings /tmp/tgt-advert.bin \
  | grep -oE 'no-thin|ofs-delta|report-status|side-band-64k|delete-refs|agent=[^ ]*' \
  | sort -u
```

That tells us unambiguously: did `entire`'s server actually advertise `no-thin`, or is the `git-sync` side reading the advert wrong?

Also — if you want to re-seed your credential helper after the typo:

```bash
# erase the bad one
printf 'protocol=https\nhost=localhost:8080\n\n' | git credential reject
# store the correct one
printf 'protocol=https\nhost=localhost:8080\nusername=test\npassword=password1234\n\n' \
  | git credential approve
```

content.text:
OK — that clears the picture. The `entiredb` keychain integration is already kicking in (that's why your replicate run got past auth to `replicate-target-no-thin`). If auth had been the problem you'd have seen a 401, not a capability rejection. So:

**Auth: working.** Just drop the `--target-username git` flag — it's noise when `entiredb` is handling it:

```bash
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --stats --measure-memory --json \
  $SRC $TGT | jq .
```

The real issue is that **entire's git server actually advertises `no-thin`**. That's a server-side decision, not a git-sync or auth problem. Let me check what's emitting it:

content.content: (Bash completed with no output)

content.text:
Ah — that's important context. `git-remote-entire` is a git remote helper (for URLs like `entire://...`), which means your regular `git push/clone` workflow uses a **custom transport** that entire implements. But `git-sync` does not go through any git remote helper — it uses go-git's HTTP transport directly to speak smart-HTTP with whatever the server at `https://localhost:8080/git/...` advertises.

So there are effectively two interfaces to entire's git service:

1. **`entire://` URLs via `git-remote-entire`** — custom protocol, whatever capabilities that helper negotiates. This is what `git clone entire://...` uses.
2. **`https://` URLs via smart-HTTP** — the HTTPS endpoint at `localhost:8080/git/...`. This is what `git-sync` and plain `git clone https://localhost:8080/git/...` use. This one is advertising `no-thin`.

That's why your `sync` attempts previously worked (sync tolerates `no-thin`, falls back to materialized) and replicate doesn't (it's strictly relay-only).

## Options

**A. Fix entire's HTTPS smart-HTTP endpoint to drop `no-thin`** — if the underlying storage can handle thin packs, this is the right fix. `git-sync replicate` then works against it directly.

Let me find where it's being set:

echo ---
find ../../entire -type d -name 'receive*' -o -name 'git*' 2>/dev/null | head -10

content.text:
Confirmed at the source. go-git v6's `plumbing/transport/serve.go:45`:

```go
if forPush {
    // TODO: support thin-pack
    _ = ar.Capabilities.Set(capability.NoThin)
    ...
}
```

So **go-git's receive-pack unconditionally advertises `no-thin`** because its server-side pack processing doesn't support thin-pack reference resolution yet (it's a `TODO`). `entire-server` uses go-git's transport for its HTTPS smart-HTTP endpoint, so it inherits this limitation.

This is why `sync` works against entire and `replicate` doesn't — `sync` falls back to the materialized strategy which encodes a complete pack, but `replicate` is relay-only by design and needs the target to accept thin packs.

## What this means for testing

Your local `entire` target genuinely **cannot be a replicate target today**, until go-git's receive-pack grows thin-pack support. Options in decreasing order of effort:

**A. Test replicate against something other than entire.** Any real git host supports thin packs: Gitea (in Docker), Gogs, a throwaway GitHub repo, GitLab, a local `git daemon` with proper git-core, etc. Quickest path:

```bash
# Gitea in 30 seconds
docker run -d --name gitea -p 3000:3000 -v /tmp/gitea:/data gitea/gitea:latest
# Create user + empty repo via http://localhost:3000, then:
TGT=http://localhost:3000/<user>/<repo>.git
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate --stats --json $SRC $TGT | jq .
```

**B. Fix go-git's receive-pack to process thin packs.** The `TODO: support thin-pack` comment says exactly what's missing. That's a meaningful undertaking — the server needs to resolve delta-from references against the repo's object store before or during pack ingestion. Worth doing, not a one-afternoon job.

**C. Change replicate to accept no-thin targets by requesting a non-thin pack from source.** You can ask an upload-pack server for a non-thin pack by *not* setting `thin-pack` in the fetch capabilities (or by setting `no-thin` explicitly in some variants). The source would then send a fully self-contained pack. This keeps replicate relay-only but makes it a bit less efficient. If you want, I can prototype this — it'd be a small change: when target advertises `no-thin`, omit/negate the thin request on the source side so the relayed pack works.

Option C might be the right move — it preserves replicate's "no materialization" guarantee while widening compatibility. Worth discussing before coding.

message.content: yes, add a no-thin option and just fallback to that if thin is not supported

60		// free to immediately send a pack following its first "ACK obj-id ready"
61		// message.
62		//
63		// Without no-done in the smart HTTP protocol, the server session would
64		// end and the client has to make another trip to send "done" before
65		// the server can send the pack. no-done removes the last round and
66		// thus slightly reduces latency.
67		NoDone Capability = "no-done"
68		// ThinPack is one with deltas which reference base objects not
69		// contained within the pack (but are known to exist at the receiving
70		// end). This can reduce the network traffic significantly, but it
71		// requires the receiving end to know how to "thicken" these packs by
72		// adding the missing bases to the pack.
73		//
74		// The upload-pack server advertises 'thin-pack' when it can generate
75		// and send a thin pack. A client requests the 'thin-pack' capability
76		// when it understands how to "thicken" it, notifying the server that
77		// it can receive such a pack. A client MUST NOT request the
78		// 'thin-pack' capability if it cannot turn a thin pack into a
79		// self-contained pack.
80		//
81		// Receive-pack, on the other hand, is assumed by default to be able to
82		// handle thin packs, but can ask the client not to use the feature by
83		// advertising the 'no-thin' capability. A client MUST NOT send a thin
84		// pack if the server advertises the 'no-thin' capability.
85		//
86		// The reasons for this asymmetry are historical. The receive-pack
87		// program did not exist until after the invention of thin packs, so
88		// historically the reference implementation of receive-pack always
89		// understood thin packs. Adding 'no-thin' later allowed receive-pack
90		// to disable the feature in a backwards-compatible manner.
91		ThinPack Capability = "thin-pack"
92		// NoThin is the opposite of the ThinPack capability.

content.text:
Good — go-git's upload-pack also has `TODO: Support thin-pack`, and more importantly, **our `FetchPack` doesn't request the `thin-pack` capability anywhere**. By protocol rules, a source only emits thin packs when the client explicitly asks for them. So we're *already* fetching non-thin packs — the `SupportsReplicateRelay` rejection is over-cautious for replicate's actual behavior.

So the fix is simpler than I'd first suggested: just drop the rejection. No new flag needed, no protocol changes, no risk of accidentally pushing a thin pack to a no-thin target (source won't make one).

Let me implement and flip the tests:

content.content: The file /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/planner/relay.go has been updated successfully.

lines: // SupportsReplicateRelay checks target-side capabilities required by
// replication mode before looking at planned ref actions.
+// Replicate tolerates targets that advertise "no-thin" because our
+// capability, so the source never emits a thin pack. The relayed pack is
+// always self-contained and safe to push to a no-thin receive-pack.
+// If gitproto.FetchPack ever begins requesting thin-pack, this check must
+// gain a matching fallback (omit the request, or explicitly advertise
+// NoThin on the source request) when target.NoThin is set.

content.text: Now also add a guard in `fetchPackV1`/`fetchPackV2` so the invariant is encoded in one place. That way, anyone who later adds thin-pack support sees the contract and has to update replicate's tolerance at the same time:

content.content: The file /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/gitproto/fetch.go has been updated successfully.

lines: +  // NOTE: we intentionally do not request capability.ThinPack. The relayed
+  // pack must stay self-contained because callers (e.g. replicate) forward
+  // it to receive-pack servers that may advertise "no-thin".
+  // planner.SupportsReplicateRelay depends on this invariant — if you add
+  // thin-pack support here, update that check to gate on target NoThin.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Changelog

Entries are grouped per release. Unreleased changes sit at the top.

## Unreleased

### Added

- `git-sync replicate` subcommand and `git-sync plan --mode replicate` for
  source-authoritative, relay-only replication. Divergent branches and tags
  are retargeted against the source; `--prune` deletes orphan managed refs.
  Relay-only by design: incompatible targets (e.g. advertising `no-thin`)
  fail with a "use sync instead" message rather than falling back to a
  materialized push.
- `gitsync.Client.Replicate` on the stable embedding surface.
- `gitsync.OperationMode`, `gitsync.ModeSync`, `gitsync.ModeReplicate`, and
  `SyncPolicy.Mode` for selecting the mode from library callers.

### Changed (stable API, breaking)

- The stable `SyncResult.Execution` summary has a new field layout:
  - Added `execution.operation_mode` (`"sync"` or `"replicate"`) to describe
    the high-level product mode the caller requested.
  - Renamed `execution.mode` to `execution.transfer_mode` to describe the
    low-level engine path that actually executed (`incremental-relay`,
    `materialized`, `bootstrap-relay`, `replicate`, etc.). The previous
    `mode` key is no longer emitted.

  External embedders that parse `execution.mode` must switch to
  `execution.transfer_mode`. There is no compatibility alias; the field was
  renamed in place.

- `syncer.Result` (the CLI-level JSON shape) gains a top-level
  `operation_mode` field alongside the existing relay fields. Callers
  consuming the flat CLI JSON can use it to distinguish sync vs replicate
  results.

### Fixed

- Work around a go-git v6 upload-pack bug where the server emits two
  consecutive `NAK` pktlines in stateless-RPC mode when the client sends
  haves that are not reachable from any want. The second NAK would
  otherwise be misread by the sideband demuxer as a frame with channel
  byte `'N'` ("unknown channel NAK"). `internal/gitproto.fetchPackV1` and
  `fetchToStoreV1` now drain trailing NAKs before handing off to the
  demuxer. See the corresponding go-git fix
  (`plumbing/transport: don't emit a second NAK after encoding empty ACKs`).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Architecture

`git-sync` is a remote-to-remote Git mirroring tool and library over smart HTTP.

## Product Rationale

The point of `git-sync` is not that Git mirroring is impossible without it. The point is that the usual alternatives are awkward at the exact layer operators often need:

- a full local mirror clone and mirror push is simple, but it turns remote-to-remote movement into a local storage and local bandwidth problem
- host-specific migration tools are useful, but they are not portable and they usually do not expose one consistent sync primitive across providers
- scripts around `git fetch` and `git push` can work, but they usually lack planning, explicit policy checks, stable machine-readable output, and a clean distinction between bootstrap and incremental sync

`git-sync` is meant to be that missing middle layer:

- provider-agnostic
- remote-to-remote
- automation-friendly
- explicit about safety and relay eligibility
- capable of handling both first-time seeding and repeat syncs

That is why the design leans so heavily on:

- relay-first strategies
- front-loaded validation
- typed results and JSON output
- explicit operation modes instead of a single opaque "mirror" operation

## When To Use `git-sync`

`git-sync` is most useful when the main problem is moving Git data between remotes without turning that into a persistent local clone service.

Strong fit:

- remote-to-remote migrations where bootstrap relay can avoid a full local mirror clone and re-push
- repeat sync jobs where updates are mostly fast-forward and the narrow incremental relay path is likely to apply
- automation environments that benefit from disposable runners and no persistent local repo storage
- provider-agnostic workflows where one explicit sync primitive is more useful than host-specific migration tooling
- operations that need clear mapping, prune, force, and relay-policy behavior with machine-readable output

Weaker fit:

- systems that need arbitrary complex Git reconciliation to succeed uniformly through one local full-state model
- services that already keep warm local mirrors and are willing to pay the storage and maintenance cost for that generality
- workflows that depend on broader history inspection, repo rewriting, or other local-object-heavy logic as a first-class feature

The practical tradeoff is:

- a local-clone service is the more general model
- `git-sync` is the lower-state, relay-first, operationally cheaper model

That means the tool gets most of its advantage when relay is common enough to be the normal case rather than an exceptional optimization.

## Core Decisions

### Relay First

The main product decision is to prefer pack relay over local decode/re-encode when that is safe:

- fetch a pack from source
- avoid materializing the full object graph locally when possible
- stream into target `receive-pack`

That is why bootstrap and incremental relay are explicit strategies instead of hidden optimizations.

### Explicit Strategy Split

The current product modes are:

- `sync`
  - planning plus reconciliation
- `replicate`
  - source-authoritative overwrite planning
  - relay-only execution
  - no materialized fallback; incompatible targets fail and should use `sync`

The current transfer modes are:

- `bootstrap`
  - empty-target relay
- incremental relay
  - narrow fast path for safe updates
- materialized fallback
  The fallback remains intentionally bounded: non-relay object materialization is kept in memory and guarded by an explicit object-count limit rather than being treated as unbounded.
  - decode/repack path when relay is not safe
- batched bootstrap
  - large initial migration fallback

## Package Model

- `pkg/gitsync`
  - stable public embedding API
  - typed `Probe`, `Plan`, `Sync`, and `Replicate` requests/results
  - auth and HTTP client injection for worker-style callers
- `pkg/gitsync/unstable`
  - explicitly non-stable first-party tooling surface
  - advanced controls, `Bootstrap`, `Fetch`, and CLI-oriented knobs
- `internal/gitproto`
  - smart HTTP, pkt-line, fetch/push request handling, capability negotiation
- `internal/planner`
  - desired refs, prune policy, action planning, checkpoint planning
- `internal/validation`
  - input normalization and front-loaded validation
- `internal/auth`
  - credential lookup, Entire token handling, token store behavior
- `internal/strategy/bootstrap`
  - one-shot relay bootstrap and batched bootstrap
- `internal/strategy/incremental`
  - narrow incremental relay path
- `internal/strategy/materialized`
  - local object materialization and encode/repack push
- `internal/syncer`
  - top-level orchestration and result shaping
- `internal/syncertest`
  - shared in-memory test fixtures

## Public API Boundary

The project now separates embedding concerns from first-party tooling concerns:

- `pkg/gitsync` is the stable library boundary.
  Callers express orchestration intent through typed probe, plan, sync, and replicate requests. Auth and transport are injected. Execution strategy remains internal.
- `pkg/gitsync/unstable` is the escape hatch for advanced controls.
  It exists so the CLI and benchmark tool can use batching limits, memory measurement, verbose progress, bootstrap, and fetch without widening the stable API prematurely.

The stable result contract is also intentionally worker-oriented:

- `Refs`
  per-ref outcomes and reasons
- `Counts`
  aggregate applied/skipped/blocked/deleted counts
- `Execution`
  protocol, operation mode, transfer summary, and batch summary

That split is intentional:

- external embedders should depend on `pkg/gitsync`
- first-party tools inside this repo may use `pkg/gitsync/unstable`
- strategy selection, batching heuristics, and materialized fallback controls are not yet treated as stable product contracts

## Protocol Boundaries

- Source discovery and source fetch can use protocol v2 when supported.
- Push remains on the current `receive-pack` path.
- `--protocol auto` prefers source-side v2 and falls back to v1.
- `--protocol v2` requires the source remote to negotiate v2.

Protocol v2 is used where it materially improves discovery and fetch behavior. Push stays on the existing low-level path because the tool already needs explicit command construction and streaming control there.

## Current Constraints

- smart HTTP only
- no local working tree
- explicit ref mapping, not wildcard mirroring
- objects still remain in memory for the duration of materialized paths
- batched bootstrap is intentionally narrower than normal sync

## Memory Assumptions

The relay paths and the materialized fallback have very different memory stories.

- Relay paths scale with streaming behavior.
  The source computes the pack, `git-sync` coordinates the transfer, and the target receives it directly. Large repositories are expected to stay viable primarily through bootstrap and incremental relay.
- Materialized fallback scales with the local object set that must be pushed.
  Once `git-sync` stops relaying and starts building a local push, it must hold the relevant Git objects in memory long enough to compute object closure and encode the outgoing pack.

Useful rules of thumb:

- Small branch delta fallback:
  Target already has the old branch tip, source has a few new commits, and the repo is mostly text/code.
  Memory is driven by the new commits, trees, and blobs above the target tip, not the full repo history.
  This is the most reasonable non-relay case.

- Broad fallback without shared history:
  Relay is unavailable and the target is missing most of the history or object graph behind the refs being updated.
  Memory can approach a large fraction of the pushed object set, especially if the repo contains large blobs.
  This is the risky case for the in-memory fallback.

- Ref-only delete or tiny tag case:
  Delete-only operations are effectively ref-only and do not need an object closure.
  Lightweight tag creation can also be close to ref-only when the target already has the underlying commit/tree/blob objects.
  These are cheap even without relay.

The important distinction is that "repo size" alone is not a sufficient predictor. For materialized fallback, the practical questions are:

- how many objects need to be sent
- how large the missing blobs are
- how much object overlap already exists on the target

That is why the rewrite keeps an explicit `--materialized-max-objects` guardrail. It is not a precise heap model; it is a coarse safety rail for the in-memory fallback path.

## Related Notes

- [bootstrap.md](bootstrap.md)
- [bootstrap-batching.md](bootstrap-batching.md)
- [benchmarking.md](benchmarking.md)
- [embedding.md](embedding.md)

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Let replicate relay against targets advertising no-thin

Targets built on go-git's receive-pack (including entire-server)
unconditionally advertise the no-thin capability because go-git has a
"TODO: support thin-pack" in plumbing/transport/serve.go. Replicate
previously rejected such targets with "use sync instead", which made
the mode unusable against the most common internal target.

Reconsidered the constraint: our upload-pack client
(gitproto.fetchPackV1 / fetchPackV2 / fetchToStoreV1) never sets the
"thin-pack" capability in the request. By protocol rules the source
only emits thin packs when the client explicitly asks for them, so the
pack we relay is always self-contained and safe to push to a no-thin
receive-pack. The rejection was overcautious.

Changes:

- planner.SupportsReplicateRelay no longer fails on target.NoThin. It
  returns ok with reason "replicate-target-capable-no-thin" so callers
  can still observe the distinction in logs and JSON.
- gitproto fetch code gets explicit comments at both the v1 and v2
  upload-request build sites documenting that we do not request
  thin-pack and that SupportsReplicateRelay depends on that
  invariant. Anyone adding thin-pack support later must update the
  planner check to gate on target NoThin.
- Integration test flipped: replicate against a no-thin target must
  now succeed and leave source and target heads matching, rather than
  fail with "use sync instead".
- Planner test split into "tolerates no-thin" and "rejects unknown
  capabilities" so the two branches of SupportsReplicateRelay each
  have direct coverage.
- CHANGELOG and docs/architecture.md updated to explain the new
  behavior and the thin-pack invariant.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
Committed as `1a477f8`. Now you should be able to replicate against your local `entire` target:

```bash
export SRC=https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes.git
export TGT=https://localhost:8080/git/test/git-sync-smoke-10

go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --stats --measure-memory --json \
  $SRC $TGT | jq '{operation_mode, relay, relay_reason, pushed, deleted, blocked}'
```

The JSON will show `relay_reason` including `replicate-target-capable-no-thin` when the target advertises no-thin, which is a useful observability signal if you ever need to debug pack size or transfer behavior.

One thing to watch: because the relayed pack is always full (not thin), overwrite/rewind scenarios against entire will transfer more bytes than they would against a thin-pack-capable target. For kubernetes/linux sized repos on incremental runs, the difference can be noticeable but is still bounded by the objects reachable from the refs being pushed. Not a correctness issue, just an efficiency footnote if you're benchmarking.

}

content.text:
That worked end-to-end against your local `entire` target. 61 refs pushed through the bootstrap-relay path, no blocks, no errors — the full chain (keychain auth → entiredb lookup → HTTPS → no-thin-tolerant replicate → receive-pack) is functional.

A couple of follow-ups to squeeze more out of this smoke test:

**1. Re-run to exercise the no-op fast path:**

```bash
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --stats --measure-memory --json \
  $SRC $TGT | jq '{operation_mode, pushed, skipped, relay_reason}'
```

Expect `pushed: 0`, `skipped: 61`, no receive-pack POST.

**2. Exercise the actual overwrite path** — rewind a branch on the target (or on the source) so a replicate run has to retarget:

```bash
# Find any ref + its parent commit from the source
BRANCH=$(curl -sk "$SRC/info/refs?service=git-upload-pack" | strings | grep -oE 'refs/heads/[^ ]+' | grep -v HEAD | head -1)

# Or just reset one target ref back a commit using git directly (easier than curl).
# Then re-run replicate and check for pushed>0 with non-bootstrap relay_reason.
```

If that run shows `relay_reason: "replicate-overwrite-relay"` and `pushed > 0`, you've confirmed the non-bootstrap relay path works against entire too.

**3. Benchmark note:** `--measure-memory` gives you `peak_heap_inuse_bytes` in the JSON. For 61 refs against kubernetes, this should stay reasonably flat — replicate streams rather than materializes. If you see it balloon, something's wrong. Worth logging for your own records.

Branch is in a shippable state now. Summary of what's on `soph/replicate`:

```
1a477f8 Let replicate relay against targets advertising no-thin
5b7223c Drop unused defaultOperationMode name param, lock in planner copy
416620d Document replicate API and stable-surface JSON rename
b5fdb49 Drain go-git upload-pack double-NAK before sideband demux
a9b1834 Add CLI tests for replicate subcommand and --force rejection
307d008 Add integration tests for replicate happy paths and --force rejection
3486c1b Expose replicate mode in API and CLI
f377186 Add relay-only replicate engine mode
```

Ready to push and open the PR when you are.

content.text:
That error doesn't have the `replicate relay failed: ... use sync instead` wrapping, which means it's from the **bootstrap-relay** path — same place the first run (the one that succeeded with 61 pushed) came from.

That strongly suggests replicate is still treating the target as empty. So either:

1. The first run didn't actually persist those 61 refs on the target, or
2. The second run isn't seeing them in the advertisement (different endpoint, different auth context, different user scope, etc.)

Quickest way to find out which is which:

```bash
# Verify target state directly
go run ./cmd/git-sync probe --json \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  $SRC $TGT | jq '{target_ref_count: (.target.refs | length), target_head: .target.refs["refs/heads/master"] // .target.refs["refs/heads/main"]}'
```

Also note — your last command dropped `--target-insecure-skip-tls-verify`. If that flag propagates (or doesn't) differently to auth vs. transport code, or if it affects whether `entiredb` refresh works, the token context may be different between runs. Put the flag back:

```bash
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT | jq .
```

and see if the second run now no-ops as expected.

## About the `short pkt-line 4` error itself

That's `pktline.Read` failing because `io.ReadFull` got fewer than 4 bytes when reading the pktline length prefix — i.e., the receive-pack response body is effectively empty. Either:

- Target returned HTTP 200 with an empty body (server-side bug, connection cut, or truncation), or
- Sideband demuxer consumed everything as progress/error frames and left nothing for `ReportStatus.Decode`.

To isolate, capture the raw response next time it happens:

```bash
# Re-run with verbose once we have tls-skip back in:
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate -v \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/replicate.log | tail -40
```

Verbose prints sideband progress, which often includes the server-side error message before the truncation.

My bet is on `--target-insecure-skip-tls-verify` being the difference. Entire likely serves a self-signed cert; without the flag the second connection may establish far enough to send but then abort before the response body is fully written, leaving you with an empty receive-pack response.

message.content: no wait, this was a new run with a new empty repo and the linux repo

content.text:
Ah, that reframes it — you're hitting a push-side failure on a very large bootstrap. "short pkt-line 4" means the receive-pack response body was empty (fewer than 4 bytes read for the pktline length prefix). Almost certainly the target closed the connection before replying, which happens on big pushes when the server has:

- a body size limit,
- a request timeout,
- an OOM during pack processing, or
- an upstream proxy buffering then dropping.

## Immediate diagnostic steps

```bash
# Tail the server while pushing
tail -f entire.log &   # or wherever entire writes

# Add verbose to the push side and capture sideband progress
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate -v \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate.log
```

Look at the very end of `/tmp/linux-replicate.log` for any sideband progress lines from the server, and at `entire.log` for an error/stacktrace at the same moment the client sees the truncation.

## Try bootstrap batching

`bootstrap` (which replicate falls back to for empty targets) supports splitting a huge push across multiple receive-pack POSTs. Replicate's bootstrap fallback plumbs the same config. Force smaller batches:

content.text:
Confirmed — `--batch-max-pack-bytes` is only wired to the `bootstrap` subcommand, not `replicate`. Replicate's bootstrap fallback **does** honor `Config.BatchMaxPackBytes` internally (`internal/syncer/syncer.go:758`), but there's no CLI flag to set it on `replicate`.

## Immediate workaround: use `bootstrap` for the initial seed

For a repo this big, do a batched bootstrap first, then use `replicate` for incremental updates going forward:

```bash
# 256 MB batches — gets under most server body limits.
# Adjust --max-pack-bytes up if the source's single pack > 1 GB.
time go run ./cmd/git-sync bootstrap \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --batch-max-pack-bytes 268435456 \
  --max-pack-bytes 10737418240 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT | tee /tmp/linux-bootstrap.json | jq '{pushed, batch_count, planned_batch_count, relay_reason}'

# From now on, incremental overwrites against the already-seeded target:
go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT | jq .
```

## Server-side diagnostic

Before (or while) you retry, watch the server log. `entire-server` sits behind connect-go (I saw those symbols in the binary earlier) which has body-size limits on gRPC. For a linux-sized push the culprit is almost certainly one of:

- connect-go message size limit — default 4 MiB for replies, often higher for requests but still bounded
- HTTP idle/read timeout
- OOM on the Raft-backed storage layer ingesting a 5 GB pack in one shot

```bash
grep -iE 'too large|exceeds|timeout|oom|kill|reset|cancel|abort' entire.log | tail -30
```

## Proper fix: add `--batch-max-pack-bytes` to `replicate` (and `sync`)

Would you like me to wire this flag through? Small change — the config plumbing already works, it's just the flag registration on `runSyncLike` that's missing. That way bootstrap-fallback inside replicate can batch without needing the user to drop down to the `bootstrap` subcommand.

content.text:
The log doesn't tell us much — it only shows the restart at 16:53:45 followed by a clean Ctrl-C shutdown at 16:54:18, with **zero** entries for receive-pack/push/pack. So one of:

1. The log rolled or this is a fresh session *after* your failed linux run, so we're not seeing that run's server-side activity.
2. The server was brought down mid-push (I see `Ctrl-C pressed` in the shutdown tail) — which would exactly produce "short pkt-line 4" on the client because the connection dies before receive-pack writes its report-status.

Either way, for a push this size, batching is almost certainly needed. Let me wire `--batch-max-pack-bytes` into `replicate` and `sync` so replicate's bootstrap fallback can split the push:

func (c *Client) buildBootstrapConfig(ctx context.Context, req BootstrapRequest) (syncer.Config, error) {
	source, err := c.resolveEndpoint(ctx, req.Source, gitsync.SourceRole)
	if err != nil {
		return syncer.Config{}, err
	}
	target, err := c.resolveEndpoint(ctx, req.Target, gitsync.TargetRole)
	if err != nil {
		return syncer.Config{}, err
	}
	return syncer.Config{
		Source:            source,
		Target:            target,
		HTTPClient:        c.httpClient,
		Branches:          append([]string(nil), req.Scope.Branches...),
		Mappings:          validationMappings(req.Scope.Mappings),
		IncludeTags:       req.IncludeTags,
		ShowStats:         req.Options.CollectStats,

	}
	return syncer.Config{
		Source:        source,

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Changelog

Entries are grouped per release. Unreleased changes sit at the top.

## Unreleased

### Added

- `git-sync replicate` subcommand and `git-sync plan --mode replicate` for
  source-authoritative, relay-only replication. Divergent branches and tags
  are retargeted against the source; `--prune` deletes orphan managed refs.
  Relay-only by design: no materialized fallback. Replicate works against
  targets that advertise `no-thin` (including any server built on go-git's
  receive-pack, e.g. `entire-server`) because the relayed pack is always
  self-contained — our upload-pack client never requests the `thin-pack`
  capability from the source.
- `gitsync.Client.Replicate` on the stable embedding surface.
- `gitsync.OperationMode`, `gitsync.ModeSync`, `gitsync.ModeReplicate`, and
  `SyncPolicy.Mode` for selecting the mode from library callers.

### Changed (stable API, breaking)

- The stable `SyncResult.Execution` summary has a new field layout:
  - Added `execution.operation_mode` (`"sync"` or `"replicate"`) to describe
    the high-level product mode the caller requested.
  - Renamed `execution.mode` to `execution.transfer_mode` to describe the
    low-level engine path that actually executed (`incremental-relay`,
    `materialized`, `bootstrap-relay`, `replicate`, etc.). The previous
    `mode` key is no longer emitted.

  External embedders that parse `execution.mode` must switch to
  `execution.transfer_mode`. There is no compatibility alias; the field was
  renamed in place.

- `syncer.Result` (the CLI-level JSON shape) gains a top-level
  `operation_mode` field alongside the existing relay fields. Callers
  consuming the flat CLI JSON can use it to distinguish sync vs replicate
  results.

### Fixed

- Work around a go-git v6 upload-pack bug where the server emits two
  consecutive `NAK` pktlines in stateless-RPC mode when the client sends
  haves that are not reachable from any want. The second NAK would
  otherwise be misread by the sideband demuxer as a frame with channel
  byte `'N'` ("unknown channel NAK"). `internal/gitproto.fetchPackV1` and
  `fetchToStoreV1` now drain trailing NAKs before handing off to the
  demuxer. See the corresponding go-git fix
  (`plumbing/transport: don't emit a second NAK after encoding empty ACKs`).

lines: `SyncPolicy.Mode` for selecting the mode from library callers.
+  `replicate`, and `plan`. Previously only `bootstrap` exposed them, but
+  replicate's bootstrap-fallback path internally honors both — without
+  these flags, users couldn't split a huge initial replicate push into
+  tractable receive-pack POSTs for size-limited targets. The unstable

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Wire max/batch pack byte flags into sync and replicate

Replicate against a fresh target for a repo the size of linux currently
fails with "decode report-status: invalid pkt-len found: short pkt-line 4"
because the receive-pack POST body hits a server limit (or times out)
and the target closes the connection before writing a report-status.
The fix is batching, which the bootstrap strategy already supports and
which replicate's bootstrap-fallback already plumbs to the strategy via
Config.BatchMaxPackBytes -- but the flags were only registered on the
bootstrap subcommand. Replicate and sync silently ignored them.

Changes:

- cmd/git-sync/main.go runSyncLike: register --max-pack-bytes and
  --batch-max-pack-bytes. Update the usage string for sync, replicate,
  and plan accordingly.
- pkg/gitsync/unstable/client.go buildSyncConfig: forward MaxPackBytes
  and BatchMaxPackBytes from AdvancedOptions to syncer.Config. The
  fields already existed on AdvancedOptions; only bootstrap was using
  them.
- internal/syncer/integration_test.go: add TestRun_Integration-
  ReplicateBootstrapBatchesWhenConfigured exercising a replicate call
  with BatchMaxPackBytes set, asserting it reaches the batched
  bootstrap path (batching=true, batch_count>=2) and leaves source
  and target heads matching. This guards the plumbing; regressing
  the forwarding would silently reintroduce the "flag has no effect"
  bug.
- CHANGELOG.md: document the added flags and the unstable
  buildSyncConfig forwarding.

Typical usage for a large initial replicate push:

  git-sync replicate \
    --batch-max-pack-bytes 268435456 \
    --max-pack-bytes 10737418240 \
    ...

splits the push into ~256 MiB batches, each of which the receive-pack
server processes and acknowledges before the next starts.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
Committed as `6a219a1`. Now retry the linux push with batching:

```bash
# Restart your entire dev env first if it's still down
cd ../../devenv && mise run dev &

# Back to git-sync, retry with batching. Adjust sizes to taste.
# 256 MiB per batch is usually a safe starting point:
export SRC=https://github.com/torvalds/linux.git
# (new empty target)

time go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --batch-max-pack-bytes 268435456 \
  --max-pack-bytes 10737418240 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate.log | jq '{pushed, batching, batch_count, planned_batch_count, relay_reason, peak: .measurement.peak_heap_inuse_bytes}'
```

And tail `entire.log` in another terminal so we can catch any server-side error *while* the push runs. If it still hits "short pkt-line 4", the server log will almost certainly show the root cause this time (body size limit, OOM, timeout, raft commit failure).

## Knobs to tune if 256 MiB batches are still too large

```bash
--batch-max-pack-bytes 67108864    # 64 MiB
--batch-max-pack-bytes 16777216    # 16 MiB
```

Smaller batches = more receive-pack POSTs = higher total time but lower peak memory and fewer opportunities to hit a limit.

content.text:
Short answer: with `-v` there's *some* progress (discrete milestone logs), but the server's sideband progress messages — the `Counting objects / Resolving deltas` lines git shows — are currently discarded. That's why a multi-minute push looks frozen.

## What you get today

With `-v`, `bootstrap.Execute` logs at milestone boundaries to stderr:

```
msg="bootstrap fetching refs from source" ref_count=...
msg="bootstrap pushing refs to target" ref_count=...
msg="bootstrap batch push checkpoint" ...
msg="bootstrap batch checkpoint complete" ...
msg="bootstrap batch branch finalized" branch=refs/heads/master
```

So you'll see a line when a batch starts, when it finishes, etc. — but nothing during the actual upload of each pack, and nothing about what the server is doing while it processes the batch.

## What's missing, and why

Two deliberate silencings in the code:

1. **`internal/gitproto/push.go:sendReceivePack`** demuxes sideband but passes `nil` for the progress sink — any "Counting / Compressing / Resolving" lines the server emits on channel 2 go to `/dev/null`.
2. **`buildV1UploadPackBody` / `fetchPackV2`** unconditionally send `no-progress` to the source, telling the source's upload-pack not to bother emitting progress at all.

Neither is conditional on `-v`. So even with verbose, you get no continuous feedback.

## Want me to wire it up?

Minimal change, ~15 lines:

- When `Verbose=true`, stop sending `no-progress` on upload-pack so the source emits progress.
- Route the fetch-side sideband progress channel and the push-side sideband progress channel to `os.Stderr`, prefixed with `source: ` / `target: ` so you can tell them apart.
- Default (non-verbose) stays quiet for scripted/JSON use.

That would give you a live stream during your linux push, something like:

```
source: Counting objects: 10000000, done.
source: Compressing objects: 37% (3712000/10000000)
source: Compressing objects: 100% (10000000/10000000), done.
target: Unpacking objects:  12% (1200000/10000000)
target: Resolving deltas:   58% (5800000/10000000)
```

Worth it? It's genuinely useful for anything over ~30s, and I can keep it behind `-v` so JSON/scripted callers are unaffected.

+// sideband.Demuxer.Progress. When verbose is false it returns nil so the

-// progress updates remain visible without double-printing.
+// prefixedLineWriter prepends a fixed prefix to each line of input written

+// deltas:  12%\r"); both are treated as line terminators so the next chunk
-  // Emit prefix lazily: only when we're about to write the first byte of

53	// FetchToStore fetches objects from source into the given store, using the
54	// appropriate protocol version.
55	func (s *RefService) FetchToStore(
56		ctx context.Context,
57		store storer.Storer,
58		conn *Conn,
59		desired map[plumbing.ReferenceName]DesiredRef,
60		targetRefs map[plumbing.ReferenceName]plumbing.Hash,
61	) error {
62		switch s.Protocol {
63		case "v2":
64			return fetchToStoreV2(ctx, store, conn, s.V2Caps, desired, targetRefs)
65		case "v1":
66			return fetchToStoreV1(ctx, store, conn, s.V1Adv, desired, targetRefs)
67		default:
68			return fmt.Errorf("unsupported source protocol %q", s.Protocol)
69		}
70	}
71	
72	// FetchPack fetches a packfile from source and returns the pack stream as a reader.
73	// Caller must close the returned ReadCloser.
74	func (s *RefService) FetchPack(
75		ctx context.Context,
76		conn *Conn,
77		desired map[plumbing.ReferenceName]DesiredRef,
78		targetRefs map[plumbing.ReferenceName]plumbing.Hash,
79	) (io.ReadCloser, error) {
80		switch s.Protocol {
81		case "v2":
82			return fetchPackV2(ctx, conn, s.V2Caps, desired, targetRefs)
83		case "v1":
84			return fetchPackV1(ctx, conn, s.V1Adv, desired, targetRefs)
85		default:
86			return nil, fmt.Errorf("unsupported source protocol %q", s.Protocol)
87		}
88	}
89	
90	// FetchCommitGraph fetches only the commit graph (tree:0 filter) for a ref.
91	// Requires v2 with filter support.
92	func (s *RefService) FetchCommitGraph(
93		ctx context.Context,
94		store storer.Storer,
95		conn *Conn,
96		ref DesiredRef,
97	) error {
98		if s.Protocol != "v2" {
99			return fmt.Errorf("commit graph fetch requires protocol v2")
100		}
101		if !s.V2Caps.FetchSupports("filter") {
102			return fmt.Errorf("source does not advertise fetch filter support")
103		}
104	
105		cmdArgs := []string{
106			"ofs-delta",
107			"no-progress",
108			"filter tree:0",
109			"want " + ref.SourceHash.String(),
110			"done",
111		}
112		body, err := EncodeCommand("fetch", s.V2Caps.RequestCapabilities(), cmdArgs)
113		if err != nil {
114			return err
115		}
116		reader, err := PostRPCStream(ctx, conn, transport.UploadPackService, body, true, "upload-pack fetch")
117		if err != nil {
118			return err
119		}
120		defer ioutil.CheckClose(reader, &err)
121		return storeV2FetchPack(store, reader)
122	}
123	
124	// Capabilities returns the sorted capability list for display.
125	func (s *RefService) Capabilities() []string {
126		switch s.Protocol {
127		case "v2":
128			return s.V2Caps.SortedKeys()
129		case "v1":
130			return AdvRefsCaps(s.V1Adv)
131		default:
132			return nil
133		}
134	}

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Stream sideband progress to stderr when -v is set

Long replicate and sync runs against large repos were silent between
bootstrap batch checkpoints because both the source upload-pack and the
target receive-pack sideband progress channels were being discarded.

Changes in internal/gitproto:

- push.go: sendReceivePack now takes a verbose flag and wires the
  sideband demuxer's Progress field to stderr (prefixed "target: ")
  when set. Added a prefixedLineWriter that splits on both '\n' and
  '\r' so in-place git progress updates ("Resolving deltas: 12%\r")
  remain readable when prefixed. progressSink returns nil for
  non-verbose so the demuxer allocates nothing on the hot path.
- fetch.go / refs.go: RefService gains a Verbose field. When true,
  fetchToStoreV1/V2, fetchPackV1/V2, and buildV1UploadPackBody stop
  asking the source to suppress progress (drop the "no-progress"
  upload-request capability and the "no-progress" v2 fetch arg) and
  wire the sideband demuxer's Progress to stderr (prefixed
  "source: ").
- syncer.go: newSession propagates cfg.Verbose to sourceService.Verbose
  right after constructing it. Target-side verbose already flowed
  through gitproto.NewPusher.

New tests:

- TestPrefixedLineWriter covers line splitting on '\n' and '\r',
  mid-line writes that don't emit a trailing prefix, and empty
  writes.
- TestProgressSinkNilWhenNotVerbose locks in the "no allocation
  when quiet" contract.

Existing private fetch tests updated to pass the new verbose
parameter. Commit-graph fetches (FetchCommitGraph) pass verbose=false
because they are short and not user-facing.

With -v, a replicate run now looks like:

  source: Enumerating objects: 120000, done.
  source: Counting objects: 100% (120000/120000), done.
  source: Compressing objects:  37% (44400/120000)
  ...
  target: Resolving deltas:  58% (69600/120000)
  target: Updating references: 100% (61/61), done.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
Committed as `3b98784`. Retry with verbose:

```bash
time go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate -v \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  --batch-max-pack-bytes 268435456 \
  --max-pack-bytes 10737418240 \
  --measure-memory --stats --json \
  $SRC $TGT 2>&1 | tee /tmp/linux-replicate.log
```

Now stderr will interleave live progress with the bootstrap batch logs, something like:

```
level=INFO msg="bootstrap fetching refs from source" ref_count=...
source: Enumerating objects: 120000, done.
source: Counting objects: 100% (120000/120000), done.
source: Compressing objects:  37% (44400/120000)
level=INFO msg="bootstrap batch push checkpoint" ...
target: Unpacking objects:  12% (14400/120000)
target: Resolving deltas:  58% (69600/120000)
level=INFO msg="bootstrap batch checkpoint complete" ...
```

The progress goes to stderr (prefixed `source: ` / `target: `) while the final JSON result still goes to stdout untouched, so your `| jq .` keeps working.

If you see progress climbing smoothly and then stopping at a specific percentage on the target side right before the "short pkt-line 4", that narrows the server-side failure to a specific phase (unpacking, resolving, writing refs) and will be very useful for the next diagnosis round.
